title: SEGMENT 10 — The PacBio family: RS, Sequel, Sequel II, Sequel IIe, Revio

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — The PacBio family: RS, Sequel, Sequel II, Sequel IIe, Revio
Welcome back.
We're going to spend this segment on the commercial history of PacBio, the company — the lineage of instruments, the rocky early years, the turnaround in 2019, and the position of the platform today.
The history is, frankly, one of the more dramatic stories in biotech, and it's instructive about how transformative technologies sometimes take years longer to land than their inventors expect.
PacBio — Pacific Biosciences, the company — was founded in 2004, originally under the name Nanofluidics.
The core technology, the zero-mode waveguide, came out of academic research at Cornell, in the labs of Watt Webb and Harold Craighead.
Three graduate students from those labs — Jonas Korlach, Steve Turner, and Mathieu Foquet — became the technical founding team.
The CEO from 2004 was Hugh Martin.
The company raised something on the order of four hundred million dollars in venture capital across six rounds before going public in October 2010.
That's a lot of money to raise pre-product.
PacBio's first commercial instrument, the PacBio RS, was released to limited customers in late 2010 and broadly in 2011.
It was a six-figure machine.
The chemistry generated reads averaging about a thousand bases — disappointingly short, given the long-read promise — with high error rates and modest throughput.
Early customers were enthusiastic about the principle but frustrated by the practicalities.
PacBio's stock, which had IPO'd at around fifteen dollars, drifted downward through the early 2010s.
The PacBio RS II, released in 2013, was an upgrade.
Better chemistry, longer reads — averaging ten to fifteen kilobases on the late P6 chemistry — higher throughput.
The RS II found a real if limited market in microbial genomics, where its ability to finish bacterial genomes — to close the gaps that short-read assemblers couldn't — was genuinely useful.
But human genomics was still Illumina territory, and PacBio's cost per gigabase was orders of magnitude higher.
The Sequel, released in 2015, was a more radical redesign.
New optics, new chip with a million ZMWs (up from 150,000 on the RS), much lower cost per cell.
Sequel was supposed to be the breakthrough.
It wasn't, quite.
The original Sequel chemistry was buggy and underdelivered.
Reads were shorter than promised, the chip didn't load as well as expected, and PacBio's financials deteriorated through 2016 and 2017.
Stock dropped below five dollars.
The company looked like it might fold.
Then in 2018, in what was probably the most dramatic moment in PacBio's commercial history, Illumina announced it was acquiring PacBio for one point two billion dollars.
The deal was framed as Illumina expanding into long reads.
For PacBio shareholders, it was a lifeline.
But it didn't go through.

The Federal Trade Commission, and antitrust authorities in the UK, sued to block the acquisition on competition grounds — basically arguing that Illumina was buying its main long-read competitor in order to neutralize them.
The legal fight dragged on for over a year.
In January 2020, the deal was abandoned, and Illumina paid PacBio a ninety-eight million dollar termination fee.
PacBio was on its own again.
But by then, something had changed.
In April 2019, between the Illumina deal being announced and being blocked, PacBio had released the Sequel II.
The Sequel II had a chip with eight million ZMWs — eight times the Sequel — and it was paired with a much better chemistry.
Crucially, the Sequel II was the instrument on which CCS / HiFi sequencing was first practical at scale.
The combination of high throughput and the HiFi workflow turned PacBio, almost overnight, into the right tool for human genome assembly.
The 2019-2020 period is, in retrospect, the platform's inflection point.

The Sequel II hardware plus the HiFi workflow plus the failed Illumina acquisition plus a new CEO (Christian Henry, formerly an Illumina executive, took over in 2020) — these things combined to relaunch PacBio as a serious competitor.
The stock recovered, customer interest surged, and major genome projects — the T2T consortium, the human pangenome project, the Vertebrate Genomes Project — adopted HiFi as a standard.

The Sequel IIe, released in 2020, was an incremental upgrade with onboard computing that ran CCS analysis in real time during the sequencing run, so customers received HiFi reads as the run finished rather than having to do CCS analysis in a separate offline step.
Small change, big workflow improvement.
The Revio, announced in late 2022 and shipping in 2023, is the current flagship.
Revio has chips with twenty-five million ZMWs per cell, and the instrument runs four cells simultaneously.
The throughput per instrument is around three hundred sixty gigabases per day at the high end.

Cost per human genome on Revio is in the low hundreds of dollars, which is a huge drop from where PacBio was just a few years ago — and competitive with what Illumina genomes cost when Illumina was at the same level of maturity.
PacBio claims Revio can sequence well over a thousand human genomes per year on a single instrument, where the Sequel II could do less than a hundred.

So the arc is roughly: rough start with the RS (2011), persistent struggle through Sequel (2015), near-death experience with the failed Illumina acquisition (2018-2020), turnaround with Sequel II plus HiFi (2019-2020), and now mature deployment with Revio (2023-onward).
It took the platform about a decade longer than its founders had hoped to find its market.
But it found it.
PacBio today is a profitable-trajectory company with real customer adoption and a clear technical lead in high-accuracy long reads.
The deeper lesson, if you want to take one from this history: transformative laboratory technologies often take fifteen to twenty years from first commercial product to broad market success.
The technology has to mature, the chemistry has to improve, the cost has to come down, and the applications have to catch up.
PacBio's principle — single-molecule real-time sequencing on ZMWs — was right in 2004.
The execution took until 2019 to fully deliver on it.
That's not unusual.
It's about average for a complex platform technology in the life sciences.
Now let's talk about what you do with the platform today.
